title: Borderline Personality Disorder with Non-Suicidal Self-Injury on an Inpatient Psychiatric Unit in a 24-Year-Old: Managing SPLITTING and Limit-Testing with a CONSISTENT, Unified Team Care Plan, Clear and Compassionate Limit-Setting, Ongoing Self-Harm/Suicide-Risk Assessment, Therapeutic Communication, Staff Self-Awareness (Countertransference), and Referral to Dialectical Behavior Therapy
subtitle: BORDERLINE PERSONALITY DISORDER (BPD) = a pervasive pattern of INSTABILITY in interpersonal relationships, self-image, and affect, with marked IMPULSIVITY, beginning by early adulthood. Core features: frantic efforts to avoid real or imagined ABANDONMENT; a pattern of intense, UNSTABLE relationships alternating between idealization and devaluation; IDENTITY disturbance (unstable self-image); IMPULSIVITY in ≥2 potentially self-damaging areas (spending, sex, substance use, reckless driving, binge eating); recurrent SUICIDAL behavior/gestures/threats or SELF-MUTILATING behavior; AFFECTIVE INSTABILITY/marked mood reactivity; chronic feelings of EMPTINESS; inappropriate, intense ANGER or difficulty controlling anger; and transient, stress-related PARANOID ideation or DISSOCIATION. NON-SUICIDAL SELF-INJURY (NSSI) — e.g., cutting/burning — is common in BPD and often serves to RELIEVE intense emotional pain, end dissociation/numbness, or self-punish; it is DISTINCT from suicidal intent (the goal is not death), BUT BPD also carries a substantially elevated risk of actual SUICIDE, so the nurse must assess self-harm and SUICIDE RISK SEPARATELY and repeatedly (never assume an act is 'just attention-seeking'). SPLITTING is the hallmark defense mechanism: the patient unconsciously views people/situations as ALL-GOOD or ALL-BAD and cannot integrate the two, often IDEALIZING some staff ('you're the only one who truly understands me') while DEVALUING others ('that other nurse is cruel and incompetent') — which can DIVIDE the team and lead staff to disagree about the patient or be manipulated into bending rules. The MOST IMPORTANT nursing response to splitting is a CONSISTENT, UNIFIED TEAM APPROACH: the entire team follows the SAME clearly-written CARE PLAN with the SAME limits, COMMUNICATES regularly (team huddles, handoffs, documentation) to share information and stay aligned, does NOT take sides or accept the role of the 'special'/idealized staff member, and does not allow the patient to play staff against one another. LIMIT-SETTING is central and therapeutic (not punitive): establish CLEAR, CONSISTENT, REASONABLE limits and boundaries; state them MATTER-OF-FACTLY and apply them uniformly across all staff and shifts; follow through with consistent, non-punitive consequences; AVOID power struggles, arguments, and personalizing the patient's behavior; maintain professional BOUNDARIES (no favoritism, no self-disclosure that fosters idealization, no 'special' exceptions); and present limits as caring structure, not rejection. THERAPEUTIC COMMUNICATION: be empathetic AND boundaried; VALIDATE the patient's emotions ('it makes sense you feel overwhelmed') WITHOUT endorsing maladaptive behavior; be honest, reliable, and consistent (which itself counters the fear of abandonment); avoid rejecting, shaming, punitive, or sarcastic responses; help the patient label feelings and use coping/distress-tolerance skills instead of self-harm. STAFF SELF-AWARENESS / COUNTERTRANSFERENCE: patients with BPD predictably evoke strong reactions in staff — anger/frustration, feeling manipulated, or 'rescue' fantasies/over-involvement in idealized staff — and staff may unconsciously act these out (being punitive, or being the 'special' rule-bender); recognizing and PROCESSING these reactions (supervision, team support, debriefing) is essential to maintaining a consistent therapeutic stance. SELF-HARM/SAFETY MANAGEMENT: maintain a SAFE milieu (remove/limit means of self-harm, appropriate observation level), assess suicide and self-harm risk on an ongoing basis, respond to self-injury MATTER-OF-FACTLY (provide necessary wound care without excessive attention/drama that could reinforce the behavior, while never being dismissive of risk), help the patient identify triggers/feelings and substitute coping skills, and involve the patient in safety planning. TREATMENT: DIALECTICAL BEHAVIOR THERAPY (DBT) is the gold-standard, evidence-based psychotherapy for BPD (skills in MINDFULNESS, DISTRESS TOLERANCE, EMOTION REGULATION, and INTERPERSONAL EFFECTIVENESS); other psychotherapies (mentalization-based therapy, schema therapy, transference-focused therapy) also help; PSYCHOTHERAPY is the mainstay, while MEDICATIONS are adjunctive and target specific symptoms (mood instability, impulsivity, transient psychosis) — there is no medication that 'cures' BPD, and clinicians AVOID benzodiazepines (disinhibition, dependence, overdose risk) and excessive polypharmacy; treat COMORBIDITIES (depression, PTSD, substance use, eating disorders, which are common). PROGNOSIS: with treatment (especially DBT) and over time, many patients improve substantially and achieve remission. The MANAGEMENT-OF-CARE/CARE-COORDINATION competencies are paramount here: building and maintaining a UNIFIED interdisciplinary CARE PLAN, COORDINATING and aligning the whole team (nursing across shifts, providers, social work, therapists) to deliver consistent limits and a consistent therapeutic stance, preventing staff splitting through communication, and arranging continuity (DBT/outpatient referral, follow-up). NURSING ROLE: deliver consistent, compassionate, boundaried care within a unified team plan; set and maintain clear limits; assess and manage self-harm/suicide risk; communicate therapeutically while validating emotions without reinforcing maladaptive behavior; manage personal/team countertransference; and coordinate evidence-based treatment (DBT) and follow-up

steps.q: Which of the following findings is MOST important for the nurse to recognize in this client?

opts.text: Borderline personality disorder features — abandonment fears, unstable intense relationships (idealization/devaluation), affective instability, impulsivity, and recurrent self-harm — precipitated by a relationship breakup
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognizing the BPD pattern frames the whole therapeutic approach. RN role: recognize features + plan accordingly.

opts.text: Treat the self-injury as attention-seeking and ignore it so as not to reinforce the behavior
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Dismissing self-harm as 'attention-seeking' and ignoring it is stigmatizing and unsafe (BPD has elevated suicide risk). Provide calm matter-of-fact wound care, assess risk seriously, and address the underlying distress — without excessive drama.

opts.text: Since she says you are the only nurse who truly understands her, accept that special role and grant her a few privileges the other nurses won't, to build rapport
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Accepting the idealized 'special' role and bending rules feeds splitting, undermines the team, and is not therapeutic. Redirect to the unified plan and apply consistent limits across all staff.

steps.q: Which statement BEST explains the significance of this client’s clinical findings?

opts.text: The best way to handle splitting is for the idealized nurse to provide most of the care while the devalued nurse stays away, to keep the patient calm
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Reinforcing the idealized/devalued split (and avoidance) entrenches splitting and divides the team. All staff apply the same consistent approach + communicate; no one accepts the 'special' role.

opts.text: Splitting (all-good/all-bad; idealization/devaluation) is the hallmark BPD defense, and a consistent, unified team approach with frequent communication is the most effective response
opts.rat: CORRECT — splitting + the consistency antidote. Foundation for team management.

opts.text: Firm limits should be presented as punishment so the patient learns consequences for manipulative behavior
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Limits are therapeutic structure delivered matter-of-factly and compassionately, NOT punishment; punitive framing damages the therapeutic relationship and is countertherapeutic.

steps.q: Which is the MOST likely explanation for this client’s presentation?

opts.text: Safety — maintain a safe milieu/observation, remove means of self-harm, and assess suicide and self-harm risk continuously
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Physical safety + risk assessment come first (elevated suicide risk in BPD). RN role: safety + risk assessment.

opts.text: Staff self-awareness/countertransference management to sustain a consistent, non-punitive stance
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Managing staff reactions prevents splitting and punitive/rule-bending responses. RN role: self-awareness + team support.

opts.text: Consistent, unified team approach to manage splitting — one shared care plan, same limits across staff/shifts, frequent team communication
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Consistency is the antidote to splitting and underpins all care. RN role: unified team plan + communication.

opts.text: Clear, compassionate limit-setting + therapeutic communication (validate emotions, not maladaptive behavior)
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Therapeutic structure + validating communication build trust and reduce maladaptive behavior. RN role: limits + therapeutic communication.

steps.q:
Present limits and consequences as punishment for manipulative behavior

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.text: Indicated
Not Indicated
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — limits are therapeutic structure delivered matter-of-factly, not punishment; punitive framing is countertherapeutic.

steps.q:
Provide calm, matter-of-fact wound care for self-injury without excessive attention, while taking the underlying distress and risk seriously

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.text: Indicated
opts.rat: Appropriate, non-reinforcing, non-dismissive response to NSSI. RN role: matter-of-fact care + risk assessment.

opts.text: Not Indicated

steps.q: Which action should the nurse take FIRST?

opts.text: Establish/follow a consistent, unified team care plan and communicate with the team (huddle) to align on limits and prevent splitting
opts.rat: #2 — Second. The unified plan + team alignment underpin all subsequent care. RN role: unified plan + team communication.

opts.text: Set clear, compassionate limits and use therapeutic communication (validate emotions, coach coping skills) consistently
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Deliver the consistent limits + validating communication day to day. RN role: limits + therapeutic communication.

opts.text: Ensure safety — safe milieu/observation, remove means of self-harm, and assess suicide/self-harm risk
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Safety + risk assessment precede everything. RN role: safety.

opts.text: Recognize/process staff countertransference (debrief/supervision) to sustain the consistent, non-punitive stance
opts.rat: #4 — Ongoing. Managing staff reactions keeps the approach consistent. RN role: self-awareness + team support.

steps.q:
The patient was referred to outpatient DBT with a written safety plan, comorbidity treatment, and follow-up, with adjunctive (non-benzodiazepine) medication only

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.text: Appropriate
opts.rat: Evidence-based treatment linkage + continuity + appropriate medication approach. Model handling.

opts.text: Inappropriate

steps.q:
The idealized nurse accepted the special role, granted extra privileges the other nurses wouldn't, and agreed the night nurse was incompetent

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.text: Appropriate
Inappropriate
opts.rat: ⚠️ Feeds splitting + divides the team + bends limits. Incorrect — redirect to the unified plan and apply consistent limits.
